summary: Gruber reflects on the shutdown of The Deck, the boutique advertising network founded by Jim Coudal that helped sustain independent web publishing for over a decade. He traces the history from his earliest attempts to monetize Daring Fireball through Google AdSense and t-shirts, through his partnership with Coudal and the creation of The Deck in 2006, which enabled him to write full-time. The Deck distinguished itself by refusing animated ads, user tracking, and multiple ads per page. Its demise was caused by advertising dollars shifting to social networks like Facebook and the rise of mobile. While Daring Fireball's revenue has diversified beyond The Deck, Gruber mourns the loss of what the network represented: proof that ethical, tasteful web advertising could work.
key_points: The Deck pioneered ethical web advertising: no animation, no tracking, no JavaScript payloads, and only one ad per page
The shift of ad dollars to walled social networks like Facebook and the rise of mobile impressions killed The Deck's business model
Exclusivity in advertising has tremendous value that most web publishers still don't understand, mirroring how premium print ads work
The Deck was built entirely on personal relationships and a handshake — Coudal refused to sell it because those relationships weren't his to sell
Gruber signals he needs to experiment with new revenue ideas, noting his best innovation (RSS sponsorships) started as a lark
money_quotes: We shared the same deep frustration: a sense that there had to be a way to do advertising on the web that didn't suck.
Perhaps most importantly, we never showed more than one ad per page.
Exclusivity has tremendous value, and most web publishers still haven't gotten that.
What hurts far more than the loss in revenue is the principle of the damn thing. The Deck did it right, and it worked for over a decade.
The Deck was built exclusively on close, personal relationships. I don't think those are mine to sell.
In 11 years, Jim and I never had anything more than a virtual handshake through Messages as a 'contract'.
They say don't do business with friends. My experience says otherwise — if you have the right friends.
I write for my pleasure, but publish for money.
key_insight: The Deck proved ethical, tasteful web advertising could work for a decade, but the consolidation of ad dollars into walled social networks ultimately made principled independence unsustainable.
